headline: NASA's Daring Moon Mission Will Test Two Landers in Space
meta_description: NASA plans an ambitious orbital rehearsal in 2027, testing SpaceX and Blue Origin lunar landers with astronauts aboard Orion before crewed Moon landings begin.
summary_short: In 2027, NASA astronauts will practice docking with prototype lunar landers from SpaceX and Blue Origin in orbit—a crucial rehearsal before returning humans to the Moon.
body:
NASA is preparing for humanity's return to the Moon with an unusually ambitious dress rehearsal scheduled for 2027. During the Artemis III demonstration mission, astronauts aboard the Orion spacecraft will rendezvous and dock separately with prototype lunar landers developed by SpaceX and Blue Origin. This complex orbital choreography will allow crews in space and teams on Earth to test the maneuvers and systems needed for safe crewed landings beginning in 2028. The knowledge gained will help NASA reduce risks and strengthen astronaut safety before the real lunar missions unfold.

The mission represents a remarkable coordination of effort. Three of the world's most powerful rockets will launch in rapid succession from multiple sites across the United States, testing ground crews, communication networks, and facilities in ways that have rarely been attempted. Blue Origin's test lander will include the crew cabin and avionics intended for future missions, and up to two Artemis III astronauts will actually enter it, wearing orange survival suits. SpaceX's test article, based on Starship Version 3, will dock nose-to-nose with Orion, allowing engineers to study how the two spacecraft behave as a single connected vehicle. The Starship test lander is 171 feet tall.

What makes Artemis III truly special is that it bridges the gap between preparation and action. Rather than rushing straight to a crewed landing, NASA and its commercial partners are building confidence methodically. The mission will rehearse the "dual launch campaign" architecture—where the lander launches first and waits in orbit for astronauts to arrive—that will define all future Moon missions. As Jeremy Parsons, Artemis program manager, noted, this will be "one of the most complex and ambitious missions NASA has ever undertaken." By practicing these intricate sequences now, in the relatively safer environment of orbital operations, NASA is laying the groundwork for a sustainable, long-term human presence on the Moon.
quick_facts: Artemis III scheduled for 2027, with crewed Moon landings to begin in 2028
SpaceX Starship test article will be 171 feet tall with nose-mounted docking system
Blue Origin astronauts will enter the test lander wearing orange Orion crew survival suits
Three world's most powerful rockets launching in rapid succession from U.S. sites
Mission tests dual-launch architecture for future lunar landing campaigns
